REPORT 6:
# Customer Company 52 AG — Mail SMTP AUTH outage (Legacy Outlook) & Direct Routing voice-policy misassignment (Enterprise-Sales)

Two related operational issues affected a small Sales sub-population after an organizational rework: (1) legacy Outlook MSI (Build 2019) clients could not send mail because tenant Security Defaults disabled SMTP AUTH for legacy authentication flows; and (2) Direct Routing-enabled users were assigned the tenant "Global" voice routing policy via an automated provisioning script, and that policy lacked PSTN Usage, preventing outbound PSTN calling. Troubleshooting spanned client-side checks (firewall/AV/profile recreation), hybrid mailflow validation (Send/Hybrid connector and TLS checks), Exchange Event Log analysis showing 535 SMTP AUTH failures, and coordination between support, BU Infrastructure Engineering and the Identity team to selectively re-enable SMTP AUTH via Conditional Access and to correct provisioning logic to assign an appropriate Direct Routing policy per the documented Teams voice-routing blueprint. The incidents were localized (roughly six affected users within a ~12-person Sales team) but exposed weak points in provisioning governance, group->policy mapping, and the operational interplay of tenant-level security defaults and legacy client compatibility. [Data: Entities (588,585,193,200,586); Relationships (1015,1018,301,313,1019)]

## Two distinct but co-occurring failure modes: SMTP AUTH/legacy Outlook send failures and Direct Routing voice-policy misassignment

The troubleshooting record shows two separate technical failure classes occurring in the same operational window: legacy SMTP submission/authentication failures for locally installed Outlook MSI Build 2019 clients, and Teams Direct Routing external-call failures for users assigned an incorrect voice routing policy. The Outlook issue traces to legacy clients relying on SMTP AUTH which was disabled by tenant Security Defaults; Event Log entries and client errors corroborated the authentication failures. [Data: Entities (588,585,586,604,597); Relationships (1015,1018,1037,1019)]

The voice issue originated from provisioning logic that assigned the tenant "Global" voice routing policy to new Enterprise-Sales group members; the Global policy did not reference a PSTN Usage route, so Direct Routing-enabled users had no external PSTN path until policy assignment was corrected. The provisioning change and the blueprint mismatch were explicitly recorded in the investigation. [Data: Entities (200,193,201,198,203); Relationships (301,303,313,309,307)]

## Root cause for mail send failures: Security Defaults disabled SMTP AUTH used by legacy Outlook clients

Server-side evidence in the Exchange Event Log showed "535 5.7.3 Authentication unsuccessful" tied to SMTP AUTH attempts, directly implicating authentication rejection rather than transport-level network issues. These events were used to correlate client symptoms (Outlook error 0x800CCC0E and stuck Outbox) with disabled legacy authentication. [Data: Entities (604,588,585,597); Relationships (1018,1015,1037,1004)]

The tenant-level Security Default setting was recently enabled and is recorded as the action that disabled legacy authentication flows including SMTP AUTH; this tenant change therefore produced the observed outage for legacy clients until selective exceptions were applied. This link between Security Defaults and legacy authentication is documented in the relationship mapping. [Data: Entities (586,608); Relationships (1019)]

## Remediation path for SMTP issue: targeted re-enablement of SMTP AUTH via Conditional Access and engineering coordination

Support and escalation engineers validated client configuration (SMTP port 587/STARTTLS, local firewall, antivirus SMTP scanning, and Outlook profile recreation) to rule out local causes before concluding the tenant-level setting was at fault. That validation path is documented and reflects standard troubleshooting discipline for auth/submission failures. [Data: Entities (609,601,602,605,588); Relationships (1024,1025,1026,1028)]

The practical remediation was to selectively allow legacy SMTP AUTH for the affected users by implementing Conditional Access adjustments while preserving tenant security posture for other users. System Engineer 07 and BU Infrastructure Engineering performed the hybrid and Event Log analysis and applied changes to enable SMTP AUTH for the impacted accounts (selective CA exceptions). This action and the coordinating actors are recorded in the incident notes. [Data: Entities (592,587); Relationships (1020,1033,1032)]

## Root cause for voice outage: automated provisioning script assigned Global policy that lacked PSTN usage contrary to the Teams voice-routing blueprint

The provisioning script was configured to assign voice routing policies based on Azure AD group membership; after the reorganization new Enterprise-Sales members were being assigned the Global policy by default. The provisioning-script -> Global policy relationship and the script change history are documented as the direct mechanism that produced the misassignment. [Data: Entities (200,198,193); Relationships (301,309,311,307)]

The Global policy was observed to have no PSTN Usage entries (no External-Route), whereas the approved blueprint requires Direct Routing users be assigned a dedicated routing policy (e.g., DR-Standard) that references an External-Route PSTN Usage. The blueprint mismatch is explicitly noted and was the justification for updating the provisioning logic. Temporary reassignment to DR-Standard restored external calling in tests. [Data: Entities (201,194,196); Relationships (305,304,303)]

## Scope and operational impact: limited user count but high operational friction and cross-team involvement

The incident affected roughly six Sales users (subset of a ~12-person Sales team) who were recently reassigned into Enterprise Sales; the user-count is small, but the issue impacted both telephony availability (external calling) and mail sending for affected legacy clients. The affected-users construct and its relation to the Sales team and Enterprise-Sales group are recorded. [Data: Entities (203,197,198); Relationships (307,300)]

Despite the limited headcount, the troubleshooting required cross-discipline escalations (support -> System Engineer -> BU Infrastructure Engineering -> Identity Team) and hybrid environment checks (on-prem Exchange connectors, Event Log, Exchange Online checks), demonstrating that even small-scale failures can impose significant operational and coordination overhead. [Data: Entities (592,582,581,593); Relationships (1012,1009,1008,1033)]

## Governance and provisioning process gaps: blueprint not enforced in provisioning logic and Azure AD group lifecycle controls

The provisioning logic did not reflect the Teams voice routing blueprint; this divergence was recorded and triggered a change to the provisioning script after the incident. The ticket explicitly links the provisioning script to the blueprint and documents that the script required correction to align with the blueprint's requirement for dedicated routing policies. [Data: Entities (200,201); Relationships (313,301)]

Separately, Azure AD groups are used both for voice policy assignment and SharePoint permissions, and the environment has documented governance rules requiring dedicated groups for SharePoint permissioning and lifecycle constraints. The dual-use of groups increases risk if group naming/creation/lifecycle controls are not strictly enforced, because a miscreated group can affect multiple downstream systems. These governance constructs are present in the records. [Data: Entities (206,470); Relationships (312,762)]

## Security exposure from tenant Security Defaults and Conditional Access exceptions

Enabling Security Defaults disabled legacy authentication methods (including SMTP AUTH) — the security change was the proximate cause of the SMTP outage for legacy clients. While enabling Security Defaults strengthens tenant security, the incident demonstrates how global security toggles can break legacy workflows. The data captures this relationship. [Data: Entities (586,608); Relationships (1019)]

The mitigation chosen—selective Conditional Access exceptions to permit legacy SMTP AUTH for specific users—reintroduces a measured exception to the baseline. While operationally necessary to restore service, such exceptions should be minimized, audited, and time-boxed because they widen the attack surface by allowing legacy authentication flows. The CA adjustment and its role in remediation are recorded in the incident documentation. [Data: Entities (587,608); Relationships (1020)]

## Prioritized recommendations: short-term fixes, medium-term controls, and longer-term remediation

Short-term (<=72 hours): 1) Maintain the Conditional Access exceptions that restore SMTP AUTH only for the minimal set of impacted users and mark them for automatic review/removal; 2) Reassign affected Direct Routing users to DR-Standard (or dedicated Sales routing policy) where tests have proven it restores outbound calling. These actions map directly to the remediation steps recorded in the ticket and the successful test reassignment. [Data: Entities (587,194,203); Relationships (1020,304,301)]

Medium-term (2–8 weeks): 1) Update and gate the provisioning script so that it enforces the Teams voice-routing blueprint (policy-to-group mapping must be codified and tested); 2) Implement a provisioning-change review process with Identity Team approval for any script that alters voice routing or group->policy mappings; 3) Add automated checks that detect when a voice routing policy lacks PSTN Usage for Direct Routing-enabled users and alert owners. The need for these actions is evidenced by the provisioning-script/blueprint divergence and the identity-team coordination records. [Data: Entities (200,201,202); Relationships (313,314,305)]

Longer-term (quarterly): 1) Inventory and remediate legacy Outlook MSI clients: migrate impacted users to modern auth-capable clients or modern protocols so SMTP AUTH dependency is removed; 2) Harden tenant-level change controls so actions like enabling Security Defaults require pre-checks for legacy auth impacts or an automated impact report. The presence of legacy clients and the tenant-level Security Defaults action motivate these plans. [Data: Entities (588,586,608); Relationships (1015,1019)]

## Stakeholders, escalation pathways, and runbook recommendations

Primary stakeholders identified in the records include the Sales Team (business owner), Identity Team (provisioning/group lifecycle), BU Infrastructure Engineering (mail/Teams infrastructure), and System Engineer 07 (technical remediation/triage). These assignments and collaborations are explicitly recorded and used in the ticket escalation chain. [Data: Entities (197,202,593,592); Relationships (307,314,1033,1032)]

Recommended runbook: 1) Triage owner (first-line support) validates symptom (Outlook 0x800CCC0E / Teams call failure) and performs client checks (port, firewall, AV, profile recreation); 2) If auth errors or Event Log 535 observed, escalate to mail/infra (System Engineer) for Event Log and hybrid connector review; 3) If due to tenant-level security defaults, coordinate Identity Team to implement time-boxed Conditional Access exception and record justification; 4) If provisioning/policy mapping implicated, escalate to Identity Team and BU Infrastructure for immediate policy reassignment and follow-up change to provisioning script. These steps mirror actions and relationships documented in the incident. [Data: Entities (601,602,605,592,200); Relationships (1025,1026,1028,1032,301)]

REPORT 13:
# KF40 Portal Publication (portal.kf40.ch) — WAF/Reverse-Proxy and Firewall Operational Community

This community centers on the perimeter publishing stack (WAF / Reverse Proxy) that publishes the Customer Company 40 portal (portal.kf40.ch) and the network/firewall estate that supports user connectivity (including Teams media). A high‑priority support ticket (TI-1594408) documented an external 502 Bad Gateway outage for portal.kf40.ch while internal access remained functional. Troubleshooting showed the backend application servers and load balancer were healthy, TLS certificates were validated and not the root cause, and the outage correlated to a weekend security patch/change that reloaded a Default-Strict inspection/profile and removed a custom header rewrite rule needed to present X-Forwarded-Proto: https to the backend. Restoring the Portal-App-Template / custom header rewrite configuration per the Secure Web Publishing blueprint resolved external access. Separately, a Zurich site firewall hardening change restricted outbound UDP to ports 3478–3481 only, blocking the dynamic RTP port range and causing one‑way/no audio in Teams; a temporary port opening restored media flows and confirmed the root cause. The incident and artifacts highlight technical, process, and governance gaps around post‑patch template/profile validation, change control for perimeter devices, and runbooks for header/HTTP profile preservation during TLS inspection/termination.

## Root cause of portal 502: WAF TLS inspection plus missing/custom header rewrite rule (X-Forwarded-Proto) prevented the backend from seeing HTTPS.

External requests to portal.kf40.ch returned 502 Bad Gateway while internal access remained functional; investigation showed the reverse proxy/WAF performs TLS termination/inspection and is expected to forward X-Forwarded-Proto: https to the backend, but that header was not present when inspection was active, causing the backend to misinterpret the request and return errors. This causal chain is documented in the incident artifacts linking the REVERSE PROXY / WAF to the APPLICATION SERVER and noting the missing CUSTOM HEADER REWRITE RULE and absent X-FORWARDED-PROTO header as drivers for the 502s [Data: Entities (302,312,319); Relationships (492,499,496)].

Restoring the header rewrite behavior per the Portal-App-Template/blueprint returned external functionality, confirming the header rewrite absence (not the backend or certificate validity) was the functional trigger for failures [Data: Entities (311,300); Relationships (568,499)].

## Security patch/template reload replaced Portal-App-Template with Default-Strict, causing template/profile drift and header removal.

A weekend security patch caused the reverse proxy/WAF to reload a default inspection/profile (Default-Strict) which replaced the custom PORTAL-APP-TEMPLATE and its documented HTTP/profile/header‑rewrite rules; the activated Default-Strict profile altered header-handling and removed or failed to preserve X-Forwarded headers, reproducing the 502 when TLS inspection was enabled [Data: Entities (310,311); Relationships (497,568,548)].

This shows configuration/template drift introduced by patching—where a device reset to a default profile overwrote application‑specific templates—rather than an application or certificate error, creating a clear process risk for perimeter devices that host per-application templates [Data: Relationships (497,562)].

## Backend and load‑balancing layer were healthy—issue isolated to publishing/inspection layer.

Load balancer health checks and internal tests marked the backend application server as healthy and reachable, indicating the backend process was not the primary failure point; monitoring and health checks were used to validate stability after the fix before ticket closure [Data: Entities (305,318,308); Relationships (504,505,513)].

These diagnostics materially constrained the fault domain to the perimeter (WAF/reverse proxy) and saved time by directing remediation to template/profile/header handling rather than application restarts alone [Data: Relationships (492,563)].

## TLS certificate change was investigated and ruled out as the root cause.

The customer reported a recently installed TLS certificate and helpdesk validated the certificate CN, SAN and chain; while the certificate change was temporally proximate to the outage, diagnostics showed certificate validity/chain issues were not the cause of the 502s—the publishing layer header behavior was [Data: Entities (306); Relationships (486)].

The certificate service and blueprint decision tree were consulted as part of the normal checklist, which helped rule TLS problems out and focus on WAF behavior [Data: Relationships (552,508)].

## Teams media outage at Zurich caused by outbound firewall restriction blocking dynamic RTP ports.

A separate but contemporaneous operational change at the Zurich site implemented a security-hardening outbound rule that permitted only UDP 3478–3481, blocking the dynamic RTP port range required for Teams media when Media Bypass is used. This change produced no audio for external calls; temporarily opening the wider UDP/RTP range restored audio immediately, confirming the firewall rule as the root cause [Data: Entities (303,442,443); Relationships (739,741,742,733)].

Firewall logs and change records tied the timing of the outbound restriction to the onset of audio issues, showing this was a local change management failure with direct impact to voice/media availability [Data: Relationships (738,739)].

## Blueprint and operational docs exist but were not enforced by the post‑patch validation process.

An authoritative Secure Web Publishing blueprint and versioned guidance (BLUEPRINT: SECURE WEB PUBLISHING – KF40 – VERSION 3.2 / WEB PUBLISHING BLUEPRINT) defines required behaviors: TLS termination at the WAF, required Portal-App-Template, and documented header rewrite rules and validation after patches. The team retrieved the blueprint from DMS to align remediation, indicating awareness of the standard; however, the blueprint's requirement to validate WAF profiles and preserve custom rewrite rules was not enforced automatically during the patch that reset profiles, producing configuration drift [Data: Entities (307,339,356); Relationships (508,512,562)].

This gap between blueprint and operational enforcement—where a profile reload caused a prohibited Default-Strict to be applied despite the blueprint forbidding it—exposes the environment to repeat regressions unless validation and change controls are hardened [Data: Relationships (548,497)].

## Operational ownership, escalation path, and remediation were used correctly but could be formalized into runbooks and automated checks.

The high-priority ticket TI-1594408 was owned initially by Employee 29 (helpdesk) who performed initial checks and escalated to BU Network & Security; BU Network & Security then took ownership and applied WAF/reverse proxy configuration changes to restore service, with monitoring confirming 24 hours stability before closure—showing correct functional escalation and remediation occurred [Data: Entities (295,298,299); Relationships (479,487,476)].

The artifacts show manual steps and handoffs (tests, template restores, monitoring) that resolved the incident, but the manual nature of these actions recommends formalizing a post‑patch WAF validation runbook, automated configuration drift detection, and explicit pre/post patch checklists to reduce mean‑time‑to‑repair and recurrence risk [Data: Relationships (479,513)].

groundedness_reason: The ticket (KF25) documents token caching/Conditional Access evaluation delays and shows investigators used Entra/Azure AD membership and sign‑in logs, supporting the first part; however, the context does not show documented AD group/object synchronization/nesting as a root cause (group nesting was present but ruled out) nor explicit evidence that ‘several’ cases required identity/BU‑Infrastructure intervention.

Ticket summary:
Ticket name: KF25: SharePoint – External project members lose access sporadically
Ticket number: TI-1583327
Company: Customer Company 25 AG
Contact person: Contact Person 07
Owner: Employee 31
Origin: Email
Type: Incident
Created on: 07.04.25 08:12
Closure
Closure date: 17.04.25 15:18
Closure text: Root cause identified (Azure AD Connect synchronization error affecting group attributes). Cleanup and full sync performed. Access stable.
Activities
07.04.25 08:04
Subject: Project members (external) lose access to SharePoint
From: Contact Person 07
Good morning
Several external project members report that they no longer have access to the folder
/Projects/Compliance_Review_2025
Internal employees do not appear to have issues.
Please review.
Kind regards
Contact Person 07
07.04.25 08:12
Ticket created
Assigned to: Employee 31
Status: In Progress
Notes
07.04.25 09:05
Helpdesk asks:
• Does it affect only external users?
• What error message appears?
• Since when exactly?
• Were groups or permissions adjusted?
07.04.25 10:20
Customer responds:
Error message:
Access denied
One user could access yesterday.
Today no longer.
No deliberate changes made.
07.04.25 11:10
Helpdesk checks SharePoint:
• Folder permissions correct
• Group “SP_Compliance_Main” contains “COMPLIANCE_TEAM”
• External users are members of “COMPLIANCE_TEAM”
Comparison internal vs external users:
No visible difference.
08.04.25 08:45
Customer adds:
One external user was newly added and immediately had access.
Another who has been in the project for months loses access sporadically.
08.04.25 10:15
Helpdesk tests:
• Remove + re-add to group
→ Access temporarily restored
After approx. 1 hour, no access again.
08.04.25 14:30
Hypothesis:
Token / cache / Conditional Access
Test:
User fully logs out → logs in again
→ Access remains denied
Hypothesis rejected.
09.04.25 09:12
Helpdesk asks:
Are external users created as “Guest” or “Member”?
09.04.25 10:05
Customer:
All externals are created as Guest.
Some were recently synchronized from On-Prem AD to Cloud.
09.04.25 11:40
First observation:
For two affected users in Entra ID portal, attribute
“source” = Windows Server AD
For functioning external users:
“source” = Azure AD
Correlation unclear.
09.04.25 14:20
Ticket escalated for in-depth analysis.
Assigned Group changed to: BU Infrastructure – Identity
10.04.25 08:55
Ticket taken over by: System Engineer 14
In-depth analysis
10.04.25 09:40
Azure AD Connect reviewed.
Observation:
Group “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” exists both On-Prem and in Cloud.
On-Prem version is synchronized.
Cloud version was originally created manually.
Result:
• External guests exist only in Cloud group
• SharePoint references the synchronized On-Prem group
Same name, but different Object IDs.
10.04.25 11:25
Review of group members via Graph:
Cloud group:
→ Contains external guests
Synchronized On-Prem group:
→ Contains only internal users
Since SharePoint evaluates by Object ID, not by name, external users lose access once synchronization takes effect.
11.04.25 08:30
Reproduction in test:
• On-Prem group re-synchronized
• Cloud group overwritten
• External users reproducibly lose access
Root Cause
Two groups existed with identical name:
COMPLIANCE_TEAM
1. Cloud-native group
2. Synchronized On-Prem group
Through Azure AD Connect, the Cloud group was gradually overwritten or deactivated.
SharePoint referenced the synchronized version.
External guests were never managed On-Prem and therefore were not included in the synchronized group.
Actions
11.04.25 – 14.04.25
• Consolidation of group structure
• Old Cloud group removed
• On-Prem group properly cleaned
• External guests managed as Cloud-only members
• Azure AD Connect full synchronization performed
15.04.25 – Monitoring
Access remains stable
No further inconsistencies
17.04.25 15:18 – Ticket closure
Dear Contact Person 07
The root cause was the parallel existence of two groups with identical names but different origins (Cloud vs. On-Prem synchronized).
Due to synchronization, effective group membership for external users was not correctly applied.
The group structure was consolidated and fully synchronized.
Access is now stable.
Kind regards
Consulting Sample Company
is_rec_reason: The statement describes a past cause and the corrective actions taken (group consolidation and a full Azure AD Connect sync) without offering any recommendation or instruction.
groundedness_reason: The ticket explicitly identifies two groups with the same name (cloud-native and synchronized On‑Prem) causing guests to lose access, and records consolidation of the group structure and a full Azure AD Connect synchronization as the remediation that restored stable access.

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: Executive S/MIME private‑key provisioning failures were remedied via PKI key‑recovery and secure PFX delivery in prior cases
context:
=== CITED REPORTS ===
REPORT 112:
# Executive S/MIME Failure — Missing Private Keys, Key-Archive Recovery, and PFX Re-provisioning

A certificate issuance/renewal process resulted in renewed S/MIME public certificates being visible in Outlook but the corresponding private keys were not present on executive devices, preventing S/MIME signing and decryption. Twelve executive mailboxes were impacted; remediation required exporting archived private keys from the PKI key-archive, packaging them as password‑protected PFX files, transmitting them via a secure channel, and importing them into each executive's Personal certificate store on workstations and managed mobile devices. Verification was performed using a sample executive account after PFX import. Key handling and manual import steps created both operational friction and sensitive key-exposure risk that should be addressed with process and automation improvements. [Data: Entities (556, 554, 568, 566, 559, 569), Relationships (1036, 1072, 1062, 1039, 1066, 1063)]

## Primary failure mode: renewed certificates without private-key provisioning broke S/MIME on executive endpoints

The executive Outlook clients displayed the newly issued S/MIME public certificates in the Trust Center but failed to perform S/MIME operations because the corresponding private keys were missing from users' Personal certificate stores; users saw Outlook errors such as "you do not have the private key" and could not sign or open encrypted/signed mail until keys were restored [Data: Entities (556, 557, 571), Relationships (1040, 1069, 1072)].

Ticket closure and triage notes link the observed Outlook behavior directly to the absence of private keys after certificate issuance, establishing the immediate root cause for the end-user impact rather than a client-configuration bug or mail-flow failure [Data: Entities (556, 554), Relationships (1036, 1072)].

## Scope and business impact: 12 executives affected; some signed/encrypted messages could not be read or validated

The incident affected a defined group of 12 executive mailboxes that relied on S/MIME for signing and encryption; these mailboxes could not perform S/MIME signing/decryption until private keys were re-provisioned locally [Data: Entities (568, 572), Relationships (1073)].

Because private keys are required both to decrypt messages addressed to the user and to create valid digital signatures, the missing keys prevented executives from opening externally signed/encrypted emails and from producing valid signed mail, representing a high-impact user-facing availability and integrity issue for executive communications [Data: Entities (554, 560), Relationships (1037)].

## Remediation path was manual/PKI-driven: key-archive recovery → PFX export → secure delivery → local import

Remediation relied on PKI key-archive recovery to retrieve the original private keys and export them into password‑protected PFX (PKCS#12) files, which were then delivered to each affected executive over a secure channel for local import [Data: Entities (555, 566, 577), Relationships (1023, 1066, 1062)].

Executives (and a sample validation account) imported the provided PFX files into their Personal certificate stores on workstations and, where applicable, managed mobile devices using a PFX import process; importing the PFX restored private-key availability and validated S/MIME signing and decryption on at least one representative account prior to incident closure [Data: Entities (569, 570, 567, 579), Relationships (1067, 1068, 1063, 1039)].

## Trust Center visibility masked the underlying private-key absence — detection gap between certificate presence and usable key material

Outlook's Trust Center showed the new public certificate in the UI, but that view does not indicate whether the private key is present in the user's Personal certificate store; this mismatch led to confusion during triage where certificates appeared in Outlook but S/MIME operations failed due to absent private keys [Data: Entities (557, 556, 571), Relationships (1040, 1069)].

This behavior identifies a detection and monitoring gap: visibility of certificate objects in client UI is insufficient to confirm serviceability for signing/decryption, so endpoint verification (Personal store checks) or automated inventory is required to detect similar failures earlier [Data: Entities (571, 569), Relationships (1069)].

## Sensitive key-handling exposure: exporting archived private keys and manual delivery increases risk

Recovery required exporting private keys into PFX files and transmitting them to users, which concentrates highly sensitive key material in transferable artifacts and across operational handoffs; while a secure channel was used, this process still increases risk compared to preserving keys only on device-protected stores or using forward-secure provisioning flows [Data: Entities (566, 577, 555), Relationships (1066, 1023)].

The need to perform out‑of‑band delivery and manual import elevates the attack surface (interception, weak PFX passwords, mishandling of exported files), so controls such as short-lived PFX, strict transfer auditing, mandatory secure channels, and minimizing human handling should be enforced and formalized [Data: Entities (566, 577), Relationships (1066)].

## Operational dependence on manual PFX import and endpoint steps is the main friction point for recovery and scale

Recovery required executing a PFX import process on each workstation and managed mobile device to restore private-key presence; this step was manual or scripted per-device, creating substantial operational overhead for a multi-user incident and a risk of inconsistent deployment across devices [Data: Entities (579, 569, 570), Relationships (1067, 1068)].

Because the PFX import was performed per-device, the process is slow to scale and liable to human error; implementing automated endpoint provisioning (via MDM, certificate auto-enrollment, or an agent-driven import combined with secure, audited delivery) would reduce time-to-repair and lower risk of misimport or missed devices [Data: Entities (579, 569, 570), Relationships (1067, 1068)].

## Verification and closure used a representative executive account but broader validation and monitoring are advisable

The recovery workflow included validating the fix using a sample executive (EXECUTIVE A) who imported a provided PFX and successfully exercised S/MIME signing/decryption, which demonstrates a partial validation approach prior to closure [Data: Entities (567), Relationships (1063)].

Relying on a single validation account confirms the recovery path but does not guarantee consistent deployment across all device types and mail clients; adding systematic post‑deployment verification (endpoint inventory, test-signed messages exchange, mobile client checks) and logging of import success/failure will provide higher assurance that all affected mailboxes are fully restored [Data: Entities (569, 570, 556), Relationships (1067, 1068, 1072)].

## Governance and technical recommendations: harden issuance pipeline, enable automation, and limit key exports

Prevent recurrence by ensuring the certificate issuance/renewal pipeline either provisions keys directly on user devices (e.g., enrollments that generate keys in device keystore) or defaults to a controlled, automated key-delivery mechanism that avoids manual PFX exports whenever possible; the current recovery pattern shows reliance on key-archive exports as a last resort [Data: Entities (554, 555, 566), Relationships (1039, 1023, 1066)].

Complement technical changes with governance: (1) require documented PKI runbooks that describe key-archival access, export approval, and secure delivery steps; (2) implement constrained, auditable PFX export policies (short-lived passwords, centralized vaulting, mandatory MFA for retrieval); (3) adopt endpoint automation (MDM or Intune-based certificate import) to reduce human handling and speed remediation [Data: Entities (555, 566, 577, 579), Relationships (1023, 1066, 1067)].

REPORT 13:
# KF80 PKI/AD: CA Rollover, Template Change and Auto‑Enrollment Impacting DC LDAPS and S/MIME

This community centers on the customer's PKI/AD estate where a weekend issuing‑CA renewal and an unintended certificate template change caused cascading authentication and TLS failures. The KF80 issuing CA (KF80-ISSUING-CA-01) was renewed but the new issuing certificate was not published to the internal AIA distribution point, producing certificate‑chain/browser warnings on internal HTTPS services (notably the HR portal). Separately, a template revision removed the Kerberos Authentication EKU for the Domain Controller Authentication template; because Group Policy–driven auto‑enrollment ran (around the ~60% renewal threshold), the four main‑site domain controllers automatically obtained certificates lacking the Kerberos EKU which caused LDAPS/TLS handshake failures for AD‑dependent applications. Executive S/MIME issues occurred when auto‑renewal/key‑archival behavior placed new public S/MIME certs into AD without delivering private keys to devices, requiring key‑recovery and re‑import. Remediation was led by BU Infrastructure (PKI/Network) and Employee 32 and included publishing the issuing CA cert to the AIA share, reverting the DC template, forcing re‑enrollment on DCs (certutil -pulse / gpupdate), re‑enrolling DC certificates from the internal CA, performing key‑archive recovery for executives, and updating runbooks and documentation for future migrations and template changes. The ticket records tie the technical actions to owners and recommend governance changes to prevent recurrence.

## Primary failure modes: missing AIA publish and an incompatible DC certificate template change

Two distinct PKI events produced separate but overlapping service impacts: (1) the renewed issuing CA certificate for KF80-ISSUING-CA-01 was not published to the internal AIA distribution point, causing chain validation failures and browser TLS warnings on internal services (for example the HR portal); and (2) a revision to the Domain Controller Authentication certificate template removed the Kerberos Authentication EKU, producing DC certificates that were incompatible with LDAPS clients and causing TLS handshake failures. These two failure modes are documented in the incident tickets and remediation notes [Data: Entities (815, 817, 841); Relationships (1557, 1613)].

The missing AIA publish is explicitly cited as the root cause for the HR portal certificate warnings and was resolved by publishing the issuing CA certificate to the AIA/CRL distribution points, after which clients could download the intermediate and clear warnings. The template change (EKU removal) is documented as the root cause for LDAPS failures and required template rollback and DC re‑enrollment to restore LDAPS connectivity [Data: Entities (815, 223, 217? ), Relationships (1557, 402, 437)].

## Auto‑enrollment + Group Policy turned a template change into a rapid, wide‑scale outage

Auto‑enrollment configured via Group Policy caused domain controllers to automatically request and install replacement certificates when they hit the configured renewal threshold (~60% lifetime). Because the Domain Controller Authentication template had been changed to remove the Kerberos EKU, the DCs auto‑enrolled certificates that were incompatible with LDAPS clients and presented them from their MY stores, producing widespread LDAPS TLS handshake failures across multiple applications. This cascade is described across the ticket timeline and is emphasized as a core contributor to incident scope and speed of impact [Data: Entities (233, 231, 215); Relationships (422, 426, 2818, 2815)].

The operational lesson is that auto-enrollment accelerates propagation of template changes and therefore template edits must be treated as high‑impact change events with pre‑deployment validation against LDAP‑S and other consuming clients to avoid mass outages [Data: Entities (1395, 233); Relationships (2799, 2818)].

## Assets and services impacted: main‑site DCs, LDAPS clients (HR, SharePoint, NAC), HR portal, VPN and executive S/MIME

The primary affected assets were the four main‑site Domain Controllers which presented the incorrect certificates from their MY stores and became the proximate cause of LDAPS handshake failures; remediation targeted these DCs directly. Internal applications that bind over LDAPS (HR provisioning system, SharePoint farm, NAC) experienced authentication or TLS failures until DC certificates were corrected; the HR portal additionally displayed browser certificate chain warnings due to the missing issuing CA on clients. The customer also reported VPN failures for ~80 remote/home users and S/MIME signing/decryption failures for 12 executives, indicating both directory‑access impacts and email security impacts across user groups [Data: Entities (226, 215, 218, 817, 211, 553); Relationships (389, 391, 1557, 1018)].

Ticket records explicitly connect the AD/LDAPS failures to both application outages and user impacts, and TI-1700005 / TI-1700008 / TI-1700007 document multiple service and executive‑level impacts that required coordinated remediation [Data: Entities (574, 210, 551); Relationships (375, 1548, 1018)].

## Remediation actions and operational owners were clear and effective but required manual interventions

Remediation was led by Employee 32 (BU Infrastructure - PKI/Network) in coordination with the customer's PKI team: steps included publishing the issuing CA certificate to the AIA share, forcing client intermediate store refreshes (gpupdate /force and certutil -pulse), reverting the Domain Controller Authentication template to restore the Kerberos EKU, removing broken DC certificates from the MY store, and re‑enrolling DC certificates from the internal CA. Executive S/MIME private keys were recovered from the CA key archive and re‑imported to devices to restore signing/decryption functionality. These steps and the responsible actors are recorded in the ticket summaries and relationships [Data: Entities (213, 232, 846, 244, 841, 555); Relationships (1604, 1606, 423, 399, 1023)].

Although the resolution sequence was effective, it relied on targeted manual actions (template rollback, certutil -pulse on each DC, key recovery/import) rather than automated, pre-validated change gates—an operational pattern that increases risk during future PKI changes [Data: Entities (244, 840); Relationships (414, 1604)].

## Missing/insufficient runbook and delivery automation amplified recovery effort

The post‑mortem and ticket closure recommend explicitly updating the PKI RUNBOOK to include AIA‑publishing steps and pre‑change LDAP‑S validation, evidence that the standard runbook lacked required prescriptive steps for CA rollover and template change testing. Similarly, the executive S/MIME issue highlighted that key archival (key archival/template setting) was enabled but the key recovery/delivery mechanism to automatically provision recovered private keys back to end devices was not defined or tested, which forced manual key‑recovery and PFX import operations. These documentation and process gaps are recorded as closure recommendations and documentation notes tied back to the PKI team and runbook updates [Data: Entities (841, 573, 563, 576); Relationships (1624, 1057, 1050)].

Updating runbooks and implementing a tested key‑recovery/delivery mechanism are explicit recommendations in the ticket and documentation entries and are the highest‑leverage controls to reduce time‑to‑recover for similar future incidents [Data: Entities (573, 576); Relationships (1051)].

## Ownership, communication and escalation paths are established but need formalized change responsibilities

Operational ownership during the incident was clear: Employee 32 and BU Infrastructure - PKI/Network led investigation and remediation, and the customer's CONTACT PERSON 31 and ADMIN (CRM) provided escalations and initial reporting. The ticket artifacts explicitly map ticket owners to remediation steps and liaison roles, indicating functional communication paths used during incident response [Data: Entities (213, 214, 212, 243); Relationships (383, 1576, 693)].

However, the incident reveals the need to formalize pre‑change responsibilities and approval gates for PKI template edits and CA rollovers (for example requiring CA publish verification and LDAP‑S compatibility testing sign‑offs), because the current operational flow allowed a template change and a missed AIA publish to progress into production without those checks [Data: Entities (232, 223, 815); Relationships (1057, 1557)].

## Security and compliance exposures: executive email integrity and broad authentication risk

The executive S/MIME failures (12 executives unable to sign/decrypt email) represent a confidentiality and non‑repudiation risk for high‑sensitivity communications (board/M&A emails) until private keys were recovered and re‑imported. Simultaneously, LDAPS failures and VPN authentication failures for remote users increased exposure by forcing fallback to less secure mechanisms or causing availability loss for authentication services. The ticket records the executive S/MIME outage and key archival/recovery workflow explicitly and ties customer business impact to these security/service‑integrity failures [Data: Entities (551, 553, 555, 211); Relationships (1023, 1034)].

Restoring proper certificate templates, ensuring correct EKUs, publishing CA artifacts to AIA, and automating key‑delivery are therefore not only availability items but also critical security controls to preserve end‑user cryptographic integrity and corporate compliance posture [Data: Entities (223, 563, 576); Relationships (1055, 1057)].

## Prioritized actionable recommendations (short‑ and medium‑term)

1) Immediate / short term: codify a mandatory pre‑deployment checklist for CA renewals and template changes that includes (a) publish new issuing/intermediate CA certs to AIA/CRL, (b) verify AIA HTTP downloads from representative client networks, (c) validate LDAP‑S binds from representative consuming apps, and (d) require runbook sign‑off by BU Infrastructure PKI owners prior to change. The tickets and runbook recommendations record these exact gaps and corrective items [Data: Entities (841, 815, 217? ), Relationships (1624, 1613, 391)].

2) Medium term: implement a tested key‑recovery/delivery automation for templates with key archival enabled (so recovered private keys can be securely provisioned to end devices), add staging validation for template EKU/usage changes, and require a controlled phased auto‑enrollment window (or disable auto‑enrollment for high‑impact templates) to avoid mass rollouts. These recommendations align with the documentation notes and template recommendations from the incident record [Data: Entities (576, 573, 563); Relationships (1051, 1057)].

3) Operational hardening: expand monitoring and alerting for anomalous certificate template churn and mass certificate renewals (triggering alerts when many DCs or services auto‑renew within a short window), and add automated post‑change validation checks (LDAPS probe, browser chain check) as part of change completion criteria. The incident’s rapid propagation via auto‑enrollment and the recovery sequence justify these controls [Data: Entities (233, 1395); Relationships (2818, 2815)].

REPORT 112:
# Executive S/MIME Failure — Missing Private Keys, Key-Archive Recovery, and PFX Re-provisioning

A certificate issuance/renewal process resulted in renewed S/MIME public certificates being visible in Outlook but the corresponding private keys were not present on executive devices, preventing S/MIME signing and decryption. Twelve executive mailboxes were impacted; remediation required exporting archived private keys from the PKI key-archive, packaging them as password‑protected PFX files, transmitting them via a secure channel, and importing them into each executive's Personal certificate store on workstations and managed mobile devices. Verification was performed using a sample executive account after PFX import. Key handling and manual import steps created both operational friction and sensitive key-exposure risk that should be addressed with process and automation improvements. [Data: Entities (556, 554, 568, 566, 559, 569), Relationships (1036, 1072, 1062, 1039, 1066, 1063)]

## Primary failure mode: renewed certificates without private-key provisioning broke S/MIME on executive endpoints

The executive Outlook clients displayed the newly issued S/MIME public certificates in the Trust Center but failed to perform S/MIME operations because the corresponding private keys were missing from users' Personal certificate stores; users saw Outlook errors such as "you do not have the private key" and could not sign or open encrypted/signed mail until keys were restored [Data: Entities (556, 557, 571), Relationships (1040, 1069, 1072)].

Ticket closure and triage notes link the observed Outlook behavior directly to the absence of private keys after certificate issuance, establishing the immediate root cause for the end-user impact rather than a client-configuration bug or mail-flow failure [Data: Entities (556, 554), Relationships (1036, 1072)].

## Scope and business impact: 12 executives affected; some signed/encrypted messages could not be read or validated

The incident affected a defined group of 12 executive mailboxes that relied on S/MIME for signing and encryption; these mailboxes could not perform S/MIME signing/decryption until private keys were re-provisioned locally [Data: Entities (568, 572), Relationships (1073)].

Because private keys are required both to decrypt messages addressed to the user and to create valid digital signatures, the missing keys prevented executives from opening externally signed/encrypted emails and from producing valid signed mail, representing a high-impact user-facing availability and integrity issue for executive communications [Data: Entities (554, 560), Relationships (1037)].

## Remediation path was manual/PKI-driven: key-archive recovery → PFX export → secure delivery → local import

Remediation relied on PKI key-archive recovery to retrieve the original private keys and export them into password‑protected PFX (PKCS#12) files, which were then delivered to each affected executive over a secure channel for local import [Data: Entities (555, 566, 577), Relationships (1023, 1066, 1062)].

Executives (and a sample validation account) imported the provided PFX files into their Personal certificate stores on workstations and, where applicable, managed mobile devices using a PFX import process; importing the PFX restored private-key availability and validated S/MIME signing and decryption on at least one representative account prior to incident closure [Data: Entities (569, 570, 567, 579), Relationships (1067, 1068, 1063, 1039)].

## Trust Center visibility masked the underlying private-key absence — detection gap between certificate presence and usable key material

Outlook's Trust Center showed the new public certificate in the UI, but that view does not indicate whether the private key is present in the user's Personal certificate store; this mismatch led to confusion during triage where certificates appeared in Outlook but S/MIME operations failed due to absent private keys [Data: Entities (557, 556, 571), Relationships (1040, 1069)].

This behavior identifies a detection and monitoring gap: visibility of certificate objects in client UI is insufficient to confirm serviceability for signing/decryption, so endpoint verification (Personal store checks) or automated inventory is required to detect similar failures earlier [Data: Entities (571, 569), Relationships (1069)].

## Sensitive key-handling exposure: exporting archived private keys and manual delivery increases risk

Recovery required exporting private keys into PFX files and transmitting them to users, which concentrates highly sensitive key material in transferable artifacts and across operational handoffs; while a secure channel was used, this process still increases risk compared to preserving keys only on device-protected stores or using forward-secure provisioning flows [Data: Entities (566, 577, 555), Relationships (1066, 1023)].

The need to perform out‑of‑band delivery and manual import elevates the attack surface (interception, weak PFX passwords, mishandling of exported files), so controls such as short-lived PFX, strict transfer auditing, mandatory secure channels, and minimizing human handling should be enforced and formalized [Data: Entities (566, 577), Relationships (1066)].

## Operational dependence on manual PFX import and endpoint steps is the main friction point for recovery and scale

Recovery required executing a PFX import process on each workstation and managed mobile device to restore private-key presence; this step was manual or scripted per-device, creating substantial operational overhead for a multi-user incident and a risk of inconsistent deployment across devices [Data: Entities (579, 569, 570), Relationships (1067, 1068)].

Because the PFX import was performed per-device, the process is slow to scale and liable to human error; implementing automated endpoint provisioning (via MDM, certificate auto-enrollment, or an agent-driven import combined with secure, audited delivery) would reduce time-to-repair and lower risk of misimport or missed devices [Data: Entities (579, 569, 570), Relationships (1067, 1068)].

## Verification and closure used a representative executive account but broader validation and monitoring are advisable

The recovery workflow included validating the fix using a sample executive (EXECUTIVE A) who imported a provided PFX and successfully exercised S/MIME signing/decryption, which demonstrates a partial validation approach prior to closure [Data: Entities (567), Relationships (1063)].

Relying on a single validation account confirms the recovery path but does not guarantee consistent deployment across all device types and mail clients; adding systematic post‑deployment verification (endpoint inventory, test-signed messages exchange, mobile client checks) and logging of import success/failure will provide higher assurance that all affected mailboxes are fully restored [Data: Entities (569, 570, 556), Relationships (1067, 1068, 1072)].

## Governance and technical recommendations: harden issuance pipeline, enable automation, and limit key exports

Prevent recurrence by ensuring the certificate issuance/renewal pipeline either provisions keys directly on user devices (e.g., enrollments that generate keys in device keystore) or defaults to a controlled, automated key-delivery mechanism that avoids manual PFX exports whenever possible; the current recovery pattern shows reliance on key-archive exports as a last resort [Data: Entities (554, 555, 566), Relationships (1039, 1023, 1066)].

Complement technical changes with governance: (1) require documented PKI runbooks that describe key-archival access, export approval, and secure delivery steps; (2) implement constrained, auditable PFX export policies (short-lived passwords, centralized vaulting, mandatory MFA for retrieval); (3) adopt endpoint automation (MDM or Intune-based certificate import) to reduce human handling and speed remediation [Data: Entities (555, 566, 577, 579), Relationships (1023, 1066, 1067)].

=== CITED REPORTS ===
REPORT 13:
# KF80 PKI/AD: CA Rollover, Template Change and Auto‑Enrollment Impacting DC LDAPS and S/MIME

This community centers on the customer's PKI/AD estate where a weekend issuing‑CA renewal and an unintended certificate template change caused cascading authentication and TLS failures. The KF80 issuing CA (KF80-ISSUING-CA-01) was renewed but the new issuing certificate was not published to the internal AIA distribution point, producing certificate‑chain/browser warnings on internal HTTPS services (notably the HR portal). Separately, a template revision removed the Kerberos Authentication EKU for the Domain Controller Authentication template; because Group Policy–driven auto‑enrollment ran (around the ~60% renewal threshold), the four main‑site domain controllers automatically obtained certificates lacking the Kerberos EKU which caused LDAPS/TLS handshake failures for AD‑dependent applications. Executive S/MIME issues occurred when auto‑renewal/key‑archival behavior placed new public S/MIME certs into AD without delivering private keys to devices, requiring key‑recovery and re‑import. Remediation was led by BU Infrastructure (PKI/Network) and Employee 32 and included publishing the issuing CA cert to the AIA share, reverting the DC template, forcing re‑enrollment on DCs (certutil -pulse / gpupdate), re‑enrolling DC certificates from the internal CA, performing key‑archive recovery for executives, and updating runbooks and documentation for future migrations and template changes. The ticket records tie the technical actions to owners and recommend governance changes to prevent recurrence.

## Primary failure modes: missing AIA publish and an incompatible DC certificate template change

Two distinct PKI events produced separate but overlapping service impacts: (1) the renewed issuing CA certificate for KF80-ISSUING-CA-01 was not published to the internal AIA distribution point, causing chain validation failures and browser TLS warnings on internal services (for example the HR portal); and (2) a revision to the Domain Controller Authentication certificate template removed the Kerberos Authentication EKU, producing DC certificates that were incompatible with LDAPS clients and causing TLS handshake failures. These two failure modes are documented in the incident tickets and remediation notes [Data: Entities (815, 817, 841); Relationships (1557, 1613)].

The missing AIA publish is explicitly cited as the root cause for the HR portal certificate warnings and was resolved by publishing the issuing CA certificate to the AIA/CRL distribution points, after which clients could download the intermediate and clear warnings. The template change (EKU removal) is documented as the root cause for LDAPS failures and required template rollback and DC re‑enrollment to restore LDAPS connectivity [Data: Entities (815, 223, 217? ), Relationships (1557, 402, 437)].

## Auto‑enrollment + Group Policy turned a template change into a rapid, wide‑scale outage

Auto‑enrollment configured via Group Policy caused domain controllers to automatically request and install replacement certificates when they hit the configured renewal threshold (~60% lifetime). Because the Domain Controller Authentication template had been changed to remove the Kerberos EKU, the DCs auto‑enrolled certificates that were incompatible with LDAPS clients and presented them from their MY stores, producing widespread LDAPS TLS handshake failures across multiple applications. This cascade is described across the ticket timeline and is emphasized as a core contributor to incident scope and speed of impact [Data: Entities (233, 231, 215); Relationships (422, 426, 2818, 2815)].

The operational lesson is that auto-enrollment accelerates propagation of template changes and therefore template edits must be treated as high‑impact change events with pre‑deployment validation against LDAP‑S and other consuming clients to avoid mass outages [Data: Entities (1395, 233); Relationships (2799, 2818)].

## Assets and services impacted: main‑site DCs, LDAPS clients (HR, SharePoint, NAC), HR portal, VPN and executive S/MIME

The primary affected assets were the four main‑site Domain Controllers which presented the incorrect certificates from their MY stores and became the proximate cause of LDAPS handshake failures; remediation targeted these DCs directly. Internal applications that bind over LDAPS (HR provisioning system, SharePoint farm, NAC) experienced authentication or TLS failures until DC certificates were corrected; the HR portal additionally displayed browser certificate chain warnings due to the missing issuing CA on clients. The customer also reported VPN failures for ~80 remote/home users and S/MIME signing/decryption failures for 12 executives, indicating both directory‑access impacts and email security impacts across user groups [Data: Entities (226, 215, 218, 817, 211, 553); Relationships (389, 391, 1557, 1018)].

Ticket records explicitly connect the AD/LDAPS failures to both application outages and user impacts, and TI-1700005 / TI-1700008 / TI-1700007 document multiple service and executive‑level impacts that required coordinated remediation [Data: Entities (574, 210, 551); Relationships (375, 1548, 1018)].

## Remediation actions and operational owners were clear and effective but required manual interventions

Remediation was led by Employee 32 (BU Infrastructure - PKI/Network) in coordination with the customer's PKI team: steps included publishing the issuing CA certificate to the AIA share, forcing client intermediate store refreshes (gpupdate /force and certutil -pulse), reverting the Domain Controller Authentication template to restore the Kerberos EKU, removing broken DC certificates from the MY store, and re‑enrolling DC certificates from the internal CA. Executive S/MIME private keys were recovered from the CA key archive and re‑imported to devices to restore signing/decryption functionality. These steps and the responsible actors are recorded in the ticket summaries and relationships [Data: Entities (213, 232, 846, 244, 841, 555); Relationships (1604, 1606, 423, 399, 1023)].

Although the resolution sequence was effective, it relied on targeted manual actions (template rollback, certutil -pulse on each DC, key recovery/import) rather than automated, pre-validated change gates—an operational pattern that increases risk during future PKI changes [Data: Entities (244, 840); Relationships (414, 1604)].

## Missing/insufficient runbook and delivery automation amplified recovery effort

The post‑mortem and ticket closure recommend explicitly updating the PKI RUNBOOK to include AIA‑publishing steps and pre‑change LDAP‑S validation, evidence that the standard runbook lacked required prescriptive steps for CA rollover and template change testing. Similarly, the executive S/MIME issue highlighted that key archival (key archival/template setting) was enabled but the key recovery/delivery mechanism to automatically provision recovered private keys back to end devices was not defined or tested, which forced manual key‑recovery and PFX import operations. These documentation and process gaps are recorded as closure recommendations and documentation notes tied back to the PKI team and runbook updates [Data: Entities (841, 573, 563, 576); Relationships (1624, 1057, 1050)].

Updating runbooks and implementing a tested key‑recovery/delivery mechanism are explicit recommendations in the ticket and documentation entries and are the highest‑leverage controls to reduce time‑to‑recover for similar future incidents [Data: Entities (573, 576); Relationships (1051)].

## Ownership, communication and escalation paths are established but need formalized change responsibilities

Operational ownership during the incident was clear: Employee 32 and BU Infrastructure - PKI/Network led investigation and remediation, and the customer's CONTACT PERSON 31 and ADMIN (CRM) provided escalations and initial reporting. The ticket artifacts explicitly map ticket owners to remediation steps and liaison roles, indicating functional communication paths used during incident response [Data: Entities (213, 214, 212, 243); Relationships (383, 1576, 693)].

However, the incident reveals the need to formalize pre‑change responsibilities and approval gates for PKI template edits and CA rollovers (for example requiring CA publish verification and LDAP‑S compatibility testing sign‑offs), because the current operational flow allowed a template change and a missed AIA publish to progress into production without those checks [Data: Entities (232, 223, 815); Relationships (1057, 1557)].

## Security and compliance exposures: executive email integrity and broad authentication risk

The executive S/MIME failures (12 executives unable to sign/decrypt email) represent a confidentiality and non‑repudiation risk for high‑sensitivity communications (board/M&A emails) until private keys were recovered and re‑imported. Simultaneously, LDAPS failures and VPN authentication failures for remote users increased exposure by forcing fallback to less secure mechanisms or causing availability loss for authentication services. The ticket records the executive S/MIME outage and key archival/recovery workflow explicitly and ties customer business impact to these security/service‑integrity failures [Data: Entities (551, 553, 555, 211); Relationships (1023, 1034)].

Restoring proper certificate templates, ensuring correct EKUs, publishing CA artifacts to AIA, and automating key‑delivery are therefore not only availability items but also critical security controls to preserve end‑user cryptographic integrity and corporate compliance posture [Data: Entities (223, 563, 576); Relationships (1055, 1057)].

## Prioritized actionable recommendations (short‑ and medium‑term)

1) Immediate / short term: codify a mandatory pre‑deployment checklist for CA renewals and template changes that includes (a) publish new issuing/intermediate CA certs to AIA/CRL, (b) verify AIA HTTP downloads from representative client networks, (c) validate LDAP‑S binds from representative consuming apps, and (d) require runbook sign‑off by BU Infrastructure PKI owners prior to change. The tickets and runbook recommendations record these exact gaps and corrective items [Data: Entities (841, 815, 217? ), Relationships (1624, 1613, 391)].

2) Medium term: implement a tested key‑recovery/delivery automation for templates with key archival enabled (so recovered private keys can be securely provisioned to end devices), add staging validation for template EKU/usage changes, and require a controlled phased auto‑enrollment window (or disable auto‑enrollment for high‑impact templates) to avoid mass rollouts. These recommendations align with the documentation notes and template recommendations from the incident record [Data: Entities (576, 573, 563); Relationships (1051, 1057)].

3) Operational hardening: expand monitoring and alerting for anomalous certificate template churn and mass certificate renewals (triggering alerts when many DCs or services auto‑renew within a short window), and add automated post‑change validation checks (LDAPS probe, browser chain check) as part of change completion criteria. The incident’s rapid propagation via auto‑enrollment and the recovery sequence justify these controls [Data: Entities (233, 1395); Relationships (2818, 2815)].

The operational lesson is that auto-enrollment accelerates propagation of template changes and therefore template edits must be treated as high‑impact change events with pre‑deployment validation against LDAP‑S and other consuming clients to avoid mass outages [Data: Entities (1395, 233); Relationships (2799, 2818)].

## Assets and services impacted: main‑site DCs, LDAPS clients (HR, SharePoint, NAC), HR portal, VPN and executive S/MIME

REPORT 112:
# Executive S/MIME Failure — Missing Private Keys, Key-Archive Recovery, and PFX Re-provisioning

A certificate issuance/renewal process resulted in renewed S/MIME public certificates being visible in Outlook but the corresponding private keys were not present on executive devices, preventing S/MIME signing and decryption. Twelve executive mailboxes were impacted; remediation required exporting archived private keys from the PKI key-archive, packaging them as password‑protected PFX files, transmitting them via a secure channel, and importing them into each executive's Personal certificate store on workstations and managed mobile devices. Verification was performed using a sample executive account after PFX import. Key handling and manual import steps created both operational friction and sensitive key-exposure risk that should be addressed with process and automation improvements. [Data: Entities (556, 554, 568, 566, 559, 569), Relationships (1036, 1072, 1062, 1039, 1066, 1063)]

## Primary failure mode: renewed certificates without private-key provisioning broke S/MIME on executive endpoints

The executive Outlook clients displayed the newly issued S/MIME public certificates in the Trust Center but failed to perform S/MIME operations because the corresponding private keys were missing from users' Personal certificate stores; users saw Outlook errors such as "you do not have the private key" and could not sign or open encrypted/signed mail until keys were restored [Data: Entities (556, 557, 571), Relationships (1040, 1069, 1072)].

Ticket closure and triage notes link the observed Outlook behavior directly to the absence of private keys after certificate issuance, establishing the immediate root cause for the end-user impact rather than a client-configuration bug or mail-flow failure [Data: Entities (556, 554), Relationships (1036, 1072)].

## Scope and business impact: 12 executives affected; some signed/encrypted messages could not be read or validated

The incident affected a defined group of 12 executive mailboxes that relied on S/MIME for signing and encryption; these mailboxes could not perform S/MIME signing/decryption until private keys were re-provisioned locally [Data: Entities (568, 572), Relationships (1073)].

Because private keys are required both to decrypt messages addressed to the user and to create valid digital signatures, the missing keys prevented executives from opening externally signed/encrypted emails and from producing valid signed mail, representing a high-impact user-facing availability and integrity issue for executive communications [Data: Entities (554, 560), Relationships (1037)].

## Remediation path was manual/PKI-driven: key-archive recovery → PFX export → secure delivery → local import

Remediation relied on PKI key-archive recovery to retrieve the original private keys and export them into password‑protected PFX (PKCS#12) files, which were then delivered to each affected executive over a secure channel for local import [Data: Entities (555, 566, 577), Relationships (1023, 1066, 1062)].

Executives (and a sample validation account) imported the provided PFX files into their Personal certificate stores on workstations and, where applicable, managed mobile devices using a PFX import process; importing the PFX restored private-key availability and validated S/MIME signing and decryption on at least one representative account prior to incident closure [Data: Entities (569, 570, 567, 579), Relationships (1067, 1068, 1063, 1039)].

## Trust Center visibility masked the underlying private-key absence — detection gap between certificate presence and usable key material

Outlook's Trust Center showed the new public certificate in the UI, but that view does not indicate whether the private key is present in the user's Personal certificate store; this mismatch led to confusion during triage where certificates appeared in Outlook but S/MIME operations failed due to absent private keys [Data: Entities (557, 556, 571), Relationships (1040, 1069)].

This behavior identifies a detection and monitoring gap: visibility of certificate objects in client UI is insufficient to confirm serviceability for signing/decryption, so endpoint verification (Personal store checks) or automated inventory is required to detect similar failures earlier [Data: Entities (571, 569), Relationships (1069)].

## Sensitive key-handling exposure: exporting archived private keys and manual delivery increases risk

Recovery required exporting private keys into PFX files and transmitting them to users, which concentrates highly sensitive key material in transferable artifacts and across operational handoffs; while a secure channel was used, this process still increases risk compared to preserving keys only on device-protected stores or using forward-secure provisioning flows [Data: Entities (566, 577, 555), Relationships (1066, 1023)].

The need to perform out‑of‑band delivery and manual import elevates the attack surface (interception, weak PFX passwords, mishandling of exported files), so controls such as short-lived PFX, strict transfer auditing, mandatory secure channels, and minimizing human handling should be enforced and formalized [Data: Entities (566, 577), Relationships (1066)].

## Operational dependence on manual PFX import and endpoint steps is the main friction point for recovery and scale

Recovery required executing a PFX import process on each workstation and managed mobile device to restore private-key presence; this step was manual or scripted per-device, creating substantial operational overhead for a multi-user incident and a risk of inconsistent deployment across devices [Data: Entities (579, 569, 570), Relationships (1067, 1068)].

Because the PFX import was performed per-device, the process is slow to scale and liable to human error; implementing automated endpoint provisioning (via MDM, certificate auto-enrollment, or an agent-driven import combined with secure, audited delivery) would reduce time-to-repair and lower risk of misimport or missed devices [Data: Entities (579, 569, 570), Relationships (1067, 1068)].

## Verification and closure used a representative executive account but broader validation and monitoring are advisable

The recovery workflow included validating the fix using a sample executive (EXECUTIVE A) who imported a provided PFX and successfully exercised S/MIME signing/decryption, which demonstrates a partial validation approach prior to closure [Data: Entities (567), Relationships (1063)].

Relying on a single validation account confirms the recovery path but does not guarantee consistent deployment across all device types and mail clients; adding systematic post‑deployment verification (endpoint inventory, test-signed messages exchange, mobile client checks) and logging of import success/failure will provide higher assurance that all affected mailboxes are fully restored [Data: Entities (569, 570, 556), Relationships (1067, 1068, 1072)].

## Governance and technical recommendations: harden issuance pipeline, enable automation, and limit key exports

Prevent recurrence by ensuring the certificate issuance/renewal pipeline either provisions keys directly on user devices (e.g., enrollments that generate keys in device keystore) or defaults to a controlled, automated key-delivery mechanism that avoids manual PFX exports whenever possible; the current recovery pattern shows reliance on key-archive exports as a last resort [Data: Entities (554, 555, 566), Relationships (1039, 1023, 1066)].

Complement technical changes with governance: (1) require documented PKI runbooks that describe key-archival access, export approval, and secure delivery steps; (2) implement constrained, auditable PFX export policies (short-lived passwords, centralized vaulting, mandatory MFA for retrieval); (3) adopt endpoint automation (MDM or Intune-based certificate import) to reduce human handling and speed remediation [Data: Entities (555, 566, 577, 579), Relationships (1023, 1066, 1067)].

=== CITED REPORTS ===
REPORT 5:
# KF70 Service Accounts — AAD Connect Sync, Conditional Access, and Scheduled Tasks

An operational migration changed OU and group membership for service accounts which caused Azure AD Connect (Entra Connect) to synchronize non-interactive service accounts into Entra ID. Those accounts became members of the KF70-AllUsers-Hybrid group used for M365 license assignment and were evaluated by a tenant baseline Conditional Access policy that required interactive MFA, resulting in scheduled-task logon failures across internal servers. Remediation included correcting AAD Connect sync/filter scope, creating a dedicated exclusion group (KF70-SERVICEACCOUNTS-NOMFA) to exempt service accounts from interactive‑MFA Conditional Access, validating scheduled tasks, and updating migration/runbooks and per-wave validation guidance to prevent recurrence. The ticket TI-1700004 documents the investigation and closure of the incident with runbook updates and operational checklists.

## AAD Connect sync-scope change (OU + group membership) unintentionally brought service accounts into Entra ID

Service accounts were moved into a new on-prem OU and that OU was included by the Azure AD Connect sync rules, which caused those previously on-prem-only accounts to be synchronized into Entra ID and become cloud-managed/hybrid objects. This sync-scope cascade is explicitly identified as the mechanism that made service accounts subject to cloud policies [Data: Entities (757, 756, 917); Relationships (1431, 1413)].

The ticket investigation links the OU movement and sync-scope inclusion to the subsequent membership/state changes that propagated into policy/licensing impact, and corrective actions included re-evaluating and correcting the AAD Connect filter to keep service accounts off cloud sync when required. The remediation steps and their rationale are recorded in the incident record TI-1700004 and associated remediation actions [Data: Entities (740, 758); Relationships (1405, 1430)].

## Conditional Access baseline policy required interactive MFA for KF70-AllUsers-Hybrid and blocked non-interactive service logons

A tenant baseline Conditional Access policy (KF70-Require-MFA) targeted the hybrid group KF70-AllUsers-Hybrid and enforced interactive MFA, which is incompatible with non-interactive service accounts used by scheduled tasks. The policy-to-group targeting and interactive‑MFA enforcement are recorded as the CA mechanism that caused authentication failures for service accounts [Data: Entities (754, 748, 747); Relationships (1422, 1410)].

Because service accounts cannot perform interactive MFA, Conditional Access evaluation caused Task Scheduler jobs and other automated authentications to fail after those accounts were synchronized into Entra ID and assigned to the targeted group; the incident narrative ties the MFA requirement directly to the observed failures [Data: Entities (740, 756); Relationships (1429, 1380)].

## Operational impact: scheduled tasks, backups, and monitoring jobs failed with Task Scheduler logon errors

Windows Task Scheduler on internal servers logged the specific failure: "Logon failure: the user has not been granted the requested logon type at this computer," and multiple scheduled jobs (reporting, backup verification, monitoring) failed to run under affected service accounts. The ticket TI-1700004 documents these operational symptoms and the use of Task Scheduler logs as primary diagnostic evidence [Data: Entities (740, 745, 744); Relationships (1380, 1402)].

Affected service accounts included named accounts (for example SVC-KF70-RPT, SVC-KF70-BKP01, SVC-KF70-MONITOR) that run across internal servers; their failures disrupted reporting, backup-verification, and monitoring tasks until remediation was applied [Data: Entities (741, 742, 743); Relationships (1384, 1390, 1396)].

## Remediation actions were effective and codified: exclusion group, sync-scope correction, and runbook updates

Remediation included creating a dedicated exclusion group (KF70-SERVICEACCOUNTS-NOMFA) and adding service accounts to it so the baseline MFA Conditional Access policy would not apply to non-interactive accounts; this exclusion pattern was implemented and recorded as part of the ticket closure activities [Data: Entities (755, 756, 754); Relationships (1428, 1422)].

In parallel, the AAD Connect sync/filter scope was corrected to prevent unintended synchronization of service accounts (reverting the OU inclusion that caused the cascade) and the migration runbook was updated to include explicit checks (exclude service accounts from CA policies, verify AAD Connect filters, validate scheduled tasks) so future waves validate non-interactive authentication as part of the wave checklist [Data: Entities (758, 762, 916); Relationships (1430, 1441, 1776)].

## Runbook and governance gaps identified: per-wave validation, rollback playbooks, and UPN/attribute handling

The incident exposed missing or insufficiently enforced per-wave validation and rollback controls; documentation and internal runbooks were updated to require explicit rollback runbooks and wave validation checklists (including scheduled task validation) to lower migration risk and enable controlled recovery [Data: Entities (915, 916, 930); Relationships (1776, 1777)].

Additional governance items include clear UPN update guidance and Entra Connect attribute handling: UPN updates and suffix changes can cascade into authentication mismatches and on-prem app mapping failures, so the guidance to apply UPN updates cloud-only unless bidirectional changes are required is relevant for future migrations [Data: Entities (912, 931, 917); Relationships (1764, 1792, 1779)].

## Attribute and licensing side-effects (mfaPreferred / license assignment) had secondary impact on authentication paths and metadata

When service accounts were synchronized and received cloud licenses, the mfaPreferred attribute interaction produced a 'small but non‑zero' effect on on-prem authentication behavior according to the incident analysis; license assignment and attribute state (mfaPreferred) were cited as contributors to observed authentication-path differences [Data: Entities (759, 760); Relationships (1439, 1434)].

The documentation also links failed authentication handshakes to application-level symptoms such as report authors resolving as 'unknown' in metadata, indicating that authentication failures can propagate beyond task execution into application data/metadata resolution [Data: Entities (1047, 1044); Relationships (2004)].

## Recommended operational controls: dedicated exclusion groups, service-account checklist, and monitoring

The incident and subsequent remediation converge on a small set of operational controls that should be standard: (1) use dedicated exclusion groups to exempt non-interactive/service accounts from interactive-MFA Conditional Access policies; (2) include scheduled-task execution and service-account authentication checks in per-wave validation checklists; and (3) verify AAD Connect OU/group filters before and after each migration wave to prevent unintended syncs. These controls are already captured as runbook updates and best-practice mappings referenced in the materials [Data: Entities (913, 930, 762); Relationships (1772, 1771, 1441)].

Implementing those controls will reduce recurrence risk because they directly address the two root causes observed here (unauthorized sync of service accounts and blanket CA targeting of a hybrid user group) and are consistent with Conditional Access best practices referenced in the documentation [Data: Entities (920, 917); Relationships (1782, 1781)].
